**Great Lent**, or the **Great Fast** ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): Μεγάλη Τεσσαρακοστή, *Megali Tessarakosti* or Μεγάλη Νηστεία, *Megali Nisteia*, meaning "Great 40 Days", and "Great Fast", respectively), is the most important [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting") season of the [church year](/wiki/Church_year "Church year") within many denominations of [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"). It is intended to prepare Christians for the greatest feast of the church year, [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter").[[1]](#cite_note-Moroz-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Eucharist_at_St._Mary%27s_Church%2C_Meenangadi.jpg/250px-Eucharist_at_St._Mary%27s_Church%2C_Meenangadi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eucharist_at_St._Mary%27s_Church,_Meenangadi.jpg)

Eucharist at St. Mary's Church, Meenangadi

Great Lent shares its origins with the [Lent of Western Christianity](/wiki/Lent "Lent") and has many similarities with it. There are some differences in the timing of Lent, besides calculating the date of Easter and how it is practiced, both liturgically in the public worship of the church and individually.

One difference between Eastern Christianity and Western Christianity is the calculation of the date of Easter (see [Computus](/wiki/Computus "Computus")). Most years, the Eastern Pascha falls after the Western Easter, and it may be as much as five weeks later; occasionally, the two dates coincide. Like Western Lent, Great Lent itself lasts for forty days, but in contrast to the West, Sundays are included in the count while [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") is not.

Great Lent officially begins on [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday"), seven weeks before [Pascha](/wiki/Easter "Easter") ([Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") is not observed in Eastern Christianity), and runs for 40 continuous days, concluding with the Presanctified Liturgy on Friday of the Sixth Week. The next day is called [Lazarus Saturday](/wiki/Lazarus_Saturday "Lazarus Saturday"), the day before [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday").

Fasting continues throughout the following week, known as Passion Week or [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), and does not end until after the [Paschal Vigil](/wiki/Paschal_Vigil "Paschal Vigil") early in the morning of Pascha (Easter Sunday).

## Purpose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Purpose")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Crucifixion_by_Theophanes_the_Cretan.jpg/250px-Crucifixion_by_Theophanes_the_Cretan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crucifixion_by_Theophanes_the_Cretan.jpg)

*The Crucifixion*. [Icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") by [Theophanes the Cretan](/wiki/Theophanes_the_Cretan "Theophanes the Cretan") (16th century, [Stavronikita monastery](/wiki/Stavronikita_monastery "Stavronikita monastery"), [Mount Athos](/wiki/Mount_Athos "Mount Athos")).

The purpose of Great Lent is to prepare the faithful to not only commemorate, but to enter into the [Passion](/wiki/Passion_(Christianity) "Passion (Christianity)") and [Resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus"). The totality of the Byzantine Rite life centers around the Resurrection.[[2]](#cite_note-LT_Meaning-2) Great Lent is intended to be a "workshop" where the character of the believer is spiritually uplifted and strengthened; where their life is rededicated to the principles and ideals of the Gospel; where [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting") and prayer culminate in deep conviction of life; where apathy and disinterest turn into vigorous activities of faith and good works.

Lent is not for the sake of Lent itself, as fasting is not for the sake of fasting. Rather, these are means by which and for which the individual believer prepares himself to reach for, accept and attain the calling of their Savior. Therefore, the significance of Great Lent is highly appraised, not only by the [monks](/wiki/Monk "Monk") who gradually increased the length of time of the Lent, but also by the [lay people](/wiki/Laity "Laity") themselves. The Eastern Orthodox lenten rules are the monastic rules. These rules exist not as a Pharisaic law, "burdens grievous to be borne" [Luke 11:46](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Luke#11:46 "s:Bible (King James)/Luke"), but as an ideal to be striven for; not as an end in themselves, but as a means to the purification of heart, the enlightening of mind, the liberation of soul and body from sin, and the spiritual perfection crowned in the virtue of love towards God and man.

In the Byzantine Rite, [asceticism](/wiki/Asceticism "Asceticism") is not exclusively for the "professional" religious, but for each layperson as well, according to their strength. As such, Great Lent is a sacred Institute of the Church to serve the individual believer in participating as a member of the [Mystical Body of Christ](/wiki/Body_of_Christ "Body of Christ"). It provides each person an annual opportunity for self-examination and improving the standards of faith and morals in their Christian life. The deep intent of the believer during Great Lent is encapsulated in the words of [Saint Paul](/wiki/Paul_of_Tarsus "Paul of Tarsus"): "forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forth unto those things which are before, I press toward the mark for the prize of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus" ([Philippians 3:13–14](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Philippians#3:13 "s:Bible (King James)/Philippians")).

Through spending more time than usual in prayer and meditation on the Holy Scripture and the [Holy Traditions](/wiki/Holy_Tradition "Holy Tradition") of the Church, the believer in Christ becomes through the grace of God more godlike. The emphasis towards this period differs somewhat from Western Christianity- the Eastern focus is less on repentance and more of an attempt to recapture humanity's original state.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The pattern of fasting and praying for 40 days is seen in the [Christian Bible](/wiki/Christian_Bible "Christian Bible"), on which basis the liturgical season of Lent was established.[[3]](#cite_note-Hynes1993-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Conte2001-4) In the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament"), the prophet [Moses](/wiki/Moses "Moses") went into the mountains for 40 days and 40 nights to pray and fast "without eating bread or drinking water" before receiving the [Ten Commandments](/wiki/Ten_Commandments "Ten Commandments") (cf. [Exodus 34:28](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Exodus#34:28 "s:Bible (King James)/Exodus")).[[4]](#cite_note-Conte2001-4) Likewise, the prophet [Elijah](/wiki/Elijah "Elijah") went into the mountains for 40 days and nights to fast and pray "until he reached Horeb, the mountain of God" when "the word of the Lord came to him" (cf. [1 Kings 19:8–9](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=1%20Kings%2019:8–9&version=nrsv)).[[4]](#cite_note-Conte2001-4) The early Christian bishop [Maximus of Turin](/wiki/Maximus_of_Turin "Maximus of Turin") wrote that as Elijah by "fasting continuously for a period of forty days and forty nights...merited to extinguish the prolonged and severe dryness of the whole world, doing so with a stream of rain and steeping the earth's dryness with the bounty of water from heaven", in the Christian tradition, this is interpreted as being "a figure of ourselves so that we, also fasting a total of forty days, might merit the spiritual rain of baptism...[and] a shower from heaven might pour down upon the dry earth of the whole world, and the abundant waters of the saving bath might saturate the lengthy drought of the Gentiles."[[5]](#cite_note-Johnson2017-5) In the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") went into the desert to fast and pray for 40 days and 40 nights; it was during this time that [Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan") tried to [tempt him](/wiki/Temptation_of_Christ "Temptation of Christ") (cf. [Matthew 4:1–3](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Matthew%204:1–3&version=nrsv)).[[4]](#cite_note-Conte2001-4) The 40-day and night fasts of Moses, Elijah, and Jesus prepared them for their work.[[3]](#cite_note-Hynes1993-3)

[Early Christianity](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity") records the tradition of the Black Fast before Easter; for the meal of the day consumed after sunset (when the fast is broken), the [Apostolic Constitutions](/wiki/Apostolic_Constitutions "Apostolic Constitutions") permit the consumption of "bread, vegetables, salt and water, in Lent" with "flesh and wine being forbidden."[[6]](#cite_note-Kellner1908-6) The [Canons of Hippolytus](/wiki/Canons_of_Hippolytus "Canons of Hippolytus") authorize only bread and salt to be consumed during [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week").[[6]](#cite_note-Kellner1908-6) The practice of fasting and abstaining from alcohol, meat and [lacticinia](/wiki/Lacticinia "Lacticinia") during Lent thus became established in the Church.[[6]](#cite_note-Kellner1908-6)

## Observance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Observance")]

### Self-discipline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Self-discipline")]

Observance of Great Lent is characterized by [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting") and [abstinence](/wiki/Abstinence "Abstinence") from certain foods, intensified private and public prayer, self-examination, [confession](/wiki/Confession_(religion) "Confession (religion)"), personal improvement, [repentance](/wiki/Repentance "Repentance") and [restitution](/wiki/Restitution "Restitution") for sins committed, and [almsgiving](/wiki/Almsgiving "Almsgiving"). Fasting is defined as not consuming food or liquids until evening (at sundown).[[7]](#cite_note-OrInf-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Samaan2024-8) In the [Coptic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church"), the Black Fast is the normative way of fasting during Great Lent; these Oriental Orthodox Christians go without water and food from midnight to sunset; after that time, the consumption of water and one [vegetarian](/wiki/Christian_vegetarianism "Christian vegetarianism") meal is permitted.[[8]](#cite_note-Samaan2024-8)

The Lenten supper that is eaten after the fast is broken in the evening must not include certain foods.[[7]](#cite_note-OrInf-7) Foods most commonly abstained from are [meat](/wiki/Meat "Meat"), [fish](/wiki/Fish "Fish"), [eggs](/wiki/Eggs_as_food "Eggs as food"), [dairy products](/wiki/Dairy_products "Dairy products"), [wine](/wiki/Wine "Wine"), and oil. According to some traditions, only [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil") is abstained from; in others, all [vegetable oils](/wiki/Vegetable_oils "Vegetable oils").[[9]](#cite_note-9) While wine and oil are permitted on Saturdays, Sundays, and a few feast days, and fish is permitted on [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") as well as the [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation") when it falls before Palm Sunday, and [caviar](/wiki/Caviar "Caviar") is permitted on [Lazarus Saturday](/wiki/Lazarus_Saturday "Lazarus Saturday"), meat and dairy are prohibited entirely until the fast is broken on [Easter (Pascha) Sunday](/wiki/Easter "Easter").[[10]](#cite_note-The_Fasting_Rule_of_the_Church-10) Additionally, married Eastern Orthodox Christians and Oriental Orthodox Christians traditionally abstain from sexual relations during Lent "to give themselves time for fasting and prayer (1 Cor. 7: 5).".[[11]](#cite_note-Menzel2014-11)[[8]](#cite_note-Samaan2024-8)

Besides the additional liturgical celebrations described below, Christians are expected to pay closer attention to and increase their private prayer.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] According to [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), when [asceticism](/wiki/Asceticism "Asceticism") is increased, prayer must be increased also. The [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers")[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] have referred to fasting without prayer as "the fast of the demons"[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] since the [demons](/wiki/Demons "Demons") do not eat according to their incorporeal nature, but neither do they pray.

### Liturgical observances

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Pope_Gregory_I.jpg/250px-Pope_Gregory_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pope_Gregory_I.jpg)

[Saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint") [Gregory Dialogus](/wiki/Gregory_Dialogus "Gregory Dialogus"), who is credited with compiling the [Liturgy of the Presanctified Gifts](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Presanctified_Gifts "Liturgy of the Presanctified Gifts")

Great Lent is unique in that, liturgically, the weeks do not run from Sunday to Saturday, but rather begin on Monday and end on Sunday, and most weeks are named for the lesson from the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") which will be read at the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") on its concluding Sunday. This is to illustrate that the entire season is anticipatory, leading up to the greatest Sunday of all: Pascha.

During the Great Fast, a special service book is used, known as the [Lenten Triodion](/wiki/Triodion "Triodion"), which contains the Lenten texts for the [Daily Office](/wiki/Daily_Office "Daily Office") (Canonical Hours) and Liturgies. The Triodion begins during the Pre-Lenten period to supplement or replace portions of the regular services. This replacement begins gradually, initially affecting only the [Epistle](/wiki/Epistle "Epistle") and [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") readings, and gradually increases until Holy Week when it entirely replaces all other liturgical material. During the [Triduum](/wiki/Triduum "Triduum") even the [Psalter](/wiki/Psalter "Psalter") is eliminated, and all texts are taken exclusively from the Triodion. The Triodion is used until the lights are extinguished before midnight at the [Paschal Vigil](/wiki/Paschal_Vigil "Paschal Vigil"), at which time it is replaced by the [Pentecostarion](/wiki/Pentecostarion "Pentecostarion"), which begins by replacing the normal services entirely (during [Bright Week](/wiki/Bright_Week "Bright Week")) and gradually diminishes until the normal services resume following the [Afterfeast](/wiki/Afterfeast "Afterfeast") of [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost").

On the weekdays of Great Lent, the full [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") is not celebrated, because the joy of the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") (literally "Thanksgiving") is contrary to the attitude of repentance which predominates on these days. Since it is considered especially important to receive the [Holy Mysteries](/wiki/Holy_Mysteries "Holy Mysteries") (Holy Communion) during this season, the [Liturgy of the Presanctified Gifts](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Presanctified_Gifts "Liturgy of the Presanctified Gifts")—also called the Liturgy of [St. Gregory the Dialogist](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_I "Pope Gregory I")— may be celebrated on weekdays.

This service starts with [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") during which [a portion](/wiki/Lamb_(liturgy) "Lamb (liturgy)") of the [Body](/wiki/Body_of_Christ "Body of Christ") and [Blood of Christ](/wiki/Blood_of_Christ "Blood of Christ"), which was [reserved](/wiki/Reserved_Sacrament "Reserved Sacrament") the previous Sunday, is brought to the [prothesis table](/wiki/Prothesis_(altar) "Prothesis (altar)"). This is followed by a solemn [great entrance](/wiki/Entrance_(liturgical)#Great_Entrance "Entrance (liturgical)") where the Holy Mysteries are brought to the altar table, and then, skipping the [anaphora (eucharistic prayer)](/wiki/Anaphora_(liturgy) "Anaphora (liturgy)"), the outline of remainder of the divine liturgy is followed, including [holy communion](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"). Most [parishes](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") and [monasteries](/wiki/Monasteries "Monasteries") celebrate this liturgy only on Wednesdays, Fridays and [feast days](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day"), but it may be celebrated on any weekday of Great Lent.

Because the divine liturgy is not celebrated on weekdays, the [Typica](/wiki/Typica#Liturgical_service "Typica") occupies its place in the [canonical hours](/wiki/Canonical_hours#Byzantine_Rite_usage "Canonical hours"), whether or not a liturgy is celebrated at vespers. On Saturday and Sunday the Divine Liturgy may be celebrated as usual. On Saturdays, the usual [Divine Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy_of_St._John_Chrysostom "Divine Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom") is celebrated; on Sundays the longer [Divine Liturgy of St. Basil the Great](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy_of_St._Basil_the_Great "Divine Liturgy of St. Basil the Great") is used.

The services of the [Canonical Hours](/wiki/Canonical_Hours "Canonical Hours") are much longer during Great Lent and the structure of the services is different on weekdays. The usual evening [small compline](/wiki/Compline#Small_Compline "Compline") is replaced by the much longer service of [Great Compline](/wiki/Great_Compline "Great Compline"). While in the Russian tradition Great Compline is used on Friday night (though some parts are read rather than sung and some Lenten material is replaced by non-Lenten hymns), in the Greek practice, ordinary Compline is used together with, on the first four weeks, a quarter of the [Akathist to the Theotokos](/wiki/Akathist#Akathist_to_the_Theotokos "Akathist"). On the fifth Saturday, known as the Saturday of the Akathist, everywhere, the entire Akathist is sung at Matins.

In “The Typikon Decoded”, Archbishop Job Getcha offers this comparison of the commemorations associated with the Sundays of Great Lent in the “Ancient Triodion”. These more ancient commemorations are retained in the hymnography still in use for the “Contemporary” Sundays of St Gregory Palamas, St John of the Ladder, and St Mary of Egypt.  On each of these, the troparia of the 1st Canon at Matins reference the more ancient commemoration, the Prodigal Son, Good Samaritan, and the Rich Man and Lazarus respectively.

During Palm Week, between the Sunday of St Mary of Egypt and Lazarus Saturday, the 1st Canon at Matins on each weekday references the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus as a parallel to the Lazarus of Bethany who falls ill and is raised from the dead after four days in the tomb (John 11:1–45).[[12]](#cite_note-12)

| Sunday of the Prodigal Son | Sunday of St Gregory Palamas |

#### Theme of Lenten joy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Theme of Lenten joy")]

A difference between the Eastern and Western observances is that while in the West the chanting of [Alleluia](/wiki/Alleluia "Alleluia") ceases during [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), in the East its use is increased. This is because for Christians, fasting should be joyous (cf. [Matthew 6:16](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#6:16 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew")), and the sense of unworthiness must always be tempered with hope in God's forgiveness.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In fact, days which follow the Lenten pattern of services are referred to as "days with Alleluia". This theme of "Lenten joy" is also found in many of the hymns of the Triodion, such as the [stichera](/wiki/Sticheron "Sticheron") which begin with the words: "The Lenten Spring has dawned!..." ([Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") [Aposticha](/wiki/Aposticha "Aposticha"), Wednesday of *[Maslenitsa](/wiki/Maslenitsa "Maslenitsa")*) and "Now is the season of repentance; let us begin it joyfully, O brethren..." ([Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins"), Second [Canon](/wiki/Canon_(hymnography) "Canon (hymnography)"), Ode 8, Monday of *Maslenitsa*).

The making of [prostrations](/wiki/Prostration "Prostration") during the services increases as well. The one [prayer](/wiki/Prayer "Prayer") that typifies the Lenten services is the [Prayer of Saint Ephrem](/wiki/Prayer_of_Saint_Ephrem "Prayer of Saint Ephrem"), which is said at each service on weekdays, accompanied by [full prostrations](/wiki/Poklon "Poklon"). One translation of it reads:

> O Lord and master of my life! a spirit of idleness, despondency, ambition and idle-talking, give me not.   
> But rather, a spirit of chastity, humble-mindedness, patience and charity, bestow upon me Thy servant.   
> Yea, my king and Lord, grant me to see my own failings and refrain from judging others: For blessed art Thou unto ages of ages. Amen.

The public reading of [Scripture](/wiki/Scripture "Scripture") is increased during Great Lent. The [Psalter](/wiki/Psalter "Psalter") ([Book of Psalms](/wiki/Book_of_Psalms "Book of Psalms")), which is normally read through once a week, is read through twice each week for the six weeks prior to Holy Week. Readings from the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") are also increased, with the Books of [Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis"), [Proverbs](/wiki/Book_of_Proverbs "Book of Proverbs") and [Isaiah](/wiki/Book_of_Isaiah "Book of Isaiah") being read through almost in their entirety at the [Sixth Hour](/wiki/Sext "Sext") and [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers"). During Cheesefare Week, the readings at these services are taken from [Joel](/wiki/Book_of_Joel "Book of Joel") and [Zechariah](/wiki/Book_of_Zechariah "Book of Zechariah"), while during Holy Week they are from [Exodus](/wiki/Book_of_Exodus "Book of Exodus"), [Ezekiel](/wiki/Book_of_Ezekiel "Book of Ezekiel") and [Job](/wiki/Book_of_Job "Book of Job").

Uniquely, on weekdays of Great Lent there is no public reading of the [Epistles](/wiki/Epistle "Epistle") or [Gospels](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel"). This is because the readings are particular to the divine liturgy, which is not celebrated on weekdays of Great Lent. There are, however, Epistles and Gospels appointed for each Saturday and Sunday.

#### Prayer for the dead

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Prayer for the dead")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/MHS_Sad_Ostateczny_XVII_w_Lipie_p.jpg/250px-MHS_Sad_Ostateczny_XVII_w_Lipie_p.jpg)](/wiki/File:MHS_Sad_Ostateczny_XVII_w_Lipie_p.jpg)

The [Last Judgment](/wiki/Last_Judgment "Last Judgment") is a repeating theme of Great Lent (17th-century icon from Lipie, [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland")).

During the Great Fast, the church also increases its [prayer for the dead](/wiki/Prayer_for_the_dead "Prayer for the dead"), not only reminding the believer of his own mortality, and thus increasing the spirit of penitence, but also to remind him of his Christian obligation of charity in praying for the departed. A number of Saturdays during Great Lent are [Saturdays of the Dead](/wiki/Saturday_of_the_dead "Saturday of the dead"), with many of the hymns of the Daily Office and at the Divine Liturgy dedicated to remembrance of the departed. These Saturdays are:

* The Saturday of Meatfare Week
* The Second Saturday of Great Lent
* The Third Saturday of Great Lent
* The Fourth Saturday of Great Lent

In addition, the [Lity](/wiki/Memorial_service_(Orthodox)#Lity "Memorial service (Orthodox)"), a brief prayer service for the departed, may be served on each weekday of Great Lent, provided there is no [feast day](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") or special observance on that day.

#### Feast days

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Feast days")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Ohrid_annunciation_icon.jpg/250px-Ohrid_annunciation_icon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ohrid_annunciation_icon.jpg)

An icon of the [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation"), the most important [feast day](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") on the [fixed calendar](/w/index.php?title=Liturgical_calendar_of_the_Byzantine_Rite&action=edit&redlink=1 "Liturgical calendar of the Byzantine Rite (page does not exist)") that can fall during Great Lent (Church of St. [Clement of Ohrid](/wiki/Clement_of_Ohrid "Clement of Ohrid"), [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia"))

Since the season of Great Lent is moveable, beginning on different dates from year to year, accommodation must be made for various feast days on the [fixed calendar](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_liturgical_calendar "Eastern Orthodox liturgical calendar") ([Menaion](/wiki/Menaion "Menaion")) which occur during the season. When these feasts fall on a weekday of Great Lent, the normal Lenten aspect of the services is lessened to celebrate the solemnity.

The most important of these fixed feasts is the [Great Feast](/wiki/Great_Feast "Great Feast") of the [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation") (March 25), which is considered to be so important that it is never moved, even if it should fall on the Sunday of Pascha itself, a rare and special occurrence which is known as *[Kyrio-Pascha](/w/index.php?title=Kyrio-Pascha&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kyrio-Pascha (page does not exist)")*. The fast is also lessened, and the faithful are allowed to eat fish, unless it is Good Friday or Holy Saturday. Whereas on other weekdays of Great Lent, no celebration of the Divine Liturgy is permitted, there is a Liturgy (usually the [Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy_of_St._John_Chrysostom "Divine Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom")) celebrated on Annunciation—even if it falls on [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday").

When the [feast day](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") of the [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of the [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") or [monastery](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") falls on a weekday of Great Lent, there is no liturgy (other than the Presanctified), but fish is allowed at the meal. In some churches the feast of a patron saint is moved to the nearest Saturday (excluding the Saturday of the Akathist), and in other churches, it is celebrated on the day of the feast itself.

When some other important feast occurs on a weekday, such as the [First and Second Finding of the Head of John the Baptist](/wiki/First_and_Second_Finding_of_the_Head_of_John_the_Baptist "First and Second Finding of the Head of John the Baptist") (February 24), the [Forty Martyrs of Sebaste](/wiki/Forty_Martyrs_of_Sebaste "Forty Martyrs of Sebaste") (March 9), etc., it is usually combined with the Lenten service, and wine and oil are allowed at the meal.

Regardless of the rank of the feast being celebrated, the Lenten hymns contained in the Triodion are never omitted, but are always chanted in their entirety, even on the feast of the Annunciation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/The_Ladder_of_Divine_Ascent_Monastery_of_St_Catherine_Sinai_12th_century.jpg/250px-The_Ladder_of_Divine_Ascent_Monastery_of_St_Catherine_Sinai_12th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Ladder_of_Divine_Ascent_Monastery_of_St_Catherine_Sinai_12th_century.jpg)

[Icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") depicting *[The Ladder of Divine Ascent](/wiki/The_Ladder_of_Divine_Ascent "The Ladder of Divine Ascent")* (12th century, [Saint Catherine's Monastery](/wiki/Saint_Catherine%27s_Monastery "Saint Catherine's Monastery"))

On the Saturdays, Sundays, and a number of weekdays during Great Lent, the service materials from the Triodion leave no room for the commemoration of the [Saint of the day](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") from the [Menaion](/wiki/Menaion "Menaion"). In order that their services not be completely forgotten, a portion of them (their canon at Matins, and their stichera from "Lord I Have Cried" at Vespers) is chanted at [Compline](/wiki/Compline "Compline").

### Readings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Readings")]

In addition to the added readings from Scripture, spiritual books by the [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers") are recommended during the Fast.

One book commonly read during Great Lent, particularly by [monastics](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism"), is *[The Ladder of Divine Ascent](/wiki/The_Ladder_of_Divine_Ascent "The Ladder of Divine Ascent")*, which was written in about the 7th century by [St. John of the Ladder](/wiki/John_of_the_Ladder "John of the Ladder") when he was the [Hegumen](/wiki/Hegumen "Hegumen") (Abbot) of [Saint Catherine's Monastery](/wiki/Saint_Catherine%27s_Monastery "Saint Catherine's Monastery") on [Mount Sinai](/wiki/Mount_Sinai "Mount Sinai"). *The Ladder* is usually read in the [trapeza](/wiki/Refectory "Refectory") (refectory) during meals, but it may alternatively be read during the [Little Hours](/wiki/Little_Hours "Little Hours") on weekdays so that everyone can hear. Many of the [laity](/wiki/Laity "Laity") also read *The Ladder* privately during Great Lent.

The theme of *The Ladder* is not Great Lent itself, but rather it deals with the ascent of the soul from earth to heaven. That is, from enslavement to the passions to the building up of the [virtues](/wiki/Virtue "Virtue") and its eventual [theosis](/wiki/Theosis_(Eastern_Orthodox_theology) "Theosis (Eastern Orthodox theology)") (union with God), which is the goal of Great Lent.

Besides the *Ladder*, in some monasteries the *[Paradise of the Holy Fathers](/wiki/Paradise_of_the_Holy_Fathers "Paradise of the Holy Fathers")* by [Palladius](/wiki/Palladius_of_Galatia "Palladius of Galatia") and the penitential [sermons](/wiki/Sermons "Sermons") of St. [Ephrem the Syrian](/wiki/Ephrem_the_Syrian "Ephrem the Syrian") are read during [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins").

## Outline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Outline")]

Main article: [Paschal cycle](/wiki/Paschal_cycle "Paschal cycle")

Liturgically, the period of the Triodion can be divided into three sections: (1) the Pre-Lenten period, (2) the Great Forty Days, and (3) Holy Week.

### Pre-Lenten period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Pre-Lenten period")]

Main article: [Pre-Lent](/wiki/Pre-Lent "Pre-Lent")

Before the forty days of Great Lent commence, there is a three-week Pre-Lenten season, to prepare the faithful for the spiritual work they are to accomplish during the Great Fast. During this period many of the themes which will be developed in the liturgical texts of the forty days are introduced. Each week runs from Monday to Sunday and is named for the Gospel theme of the Sunday which concludes it.

In the Slavic tradition, with the addition of Zacchaeus Sunday, some regard the pre-Lenten period as lasting four weeks, but there are no liturgical indications that the week following the fifth Sunday before Lent (whether preceded by Zacchaeus Sunday or otherwise) is in any way Lenten, because Zacchaeus Sunday falls outside the *[Triodion](/wiki/Triodion "Triodion")*, the [liturgical book](/wiki/Liturgical_book "Liturgical book") which governs the pre-Lenten period and Lent itself.

#### Zacchaeus Sunday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Zacchaeus Sunday")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Hole_zachaeus_in_tree.gif/250px-Hole_zachaeus_in_tree.gif)](/wiki/File:Hole_zachaeus_in_tree.gif)

[Zacchaeus](/wiki/Zacchaeus "Zacchaeus") being called down from the tree (1908, William Hole)

In the Slavic liturgical traditions, Zacchaeus Sunday occurs on the fifth Sunday before the beginning of Great Lent (which starts on a Monday). Though there are no materials provided in the Lenten Triodion for this day, it is the very first day that is affected by the date of the upcoming Pascha (all the preceding days having been affected by the previous Pascha). This day has one sole Pre-Lenten feature: the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") reading is always the account of [Zacchaeus](/wiki/Zacchaeus "Zacchaeus") from [Luke 19:1–10](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Luke#19:1 "s:Bible (King James)/Luke"), for which reason this Sunday is referred to as "Zacchaeus Sunday" (though the week before is not called "Zacchaeus week").

This reading actually falls at the end of the [lectionary](/wiki/Lectionary "Lectionary") cycle, being assigned to the 32nd Week after Pentecost. However, depending upon the date of the upcoming Pascha, the readings of the preceding weeks are either skipped (if Pascha will be early) or repeated (if it will be late) so that the readings for the 32nd Sunday after Pentecost always occur on the Sunday preceding the Week of the Publican and the Pharisee.

In the Byzantine ("Greek") liturgical traditions, the Gospel reading for Zacchaeus remains in the normal lectionary cycle and does not always fall on the fifth Sunday before Lent. In fact, it usually falls a few weeks before, and the fifth Sunday before Lent is known as the Sunday of the Canaanite Woman after the story in [Matthew 15:21–28](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#15:21 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew").

The Lenten significance of the Gospel account of Zacchaeus is that it introduces the themes of pious zeal (Zacchaeus' climbing up the sycamore tree; Jesus' words: "Zacchaeus, make haste"), restraint (Jesus' words: "come down"), making a place for Jesus in the heart ("I must abide at thy house"), overcoming gossip ("And when they saw it, they all murmured, saying, That he was gone to be guest with a man that is a sinner"), repentance and almsgiving ("And Zacchaeus stood, and said unto the Lord: Behold, Lord, the half of my goods I give to the poor; and if I have taken any thing from any man by false accusation, I restore him fourfold"), forgiveness and reconciliation ("And Jesus said unto him, This day is salvation come to this house, forsomuch as he also is a son of Abraham"), and the reason for the Passion and Resurrection ("For the Son of man is come to seek and to save that which was lost").

The [Epistle](/wiki/Epistle "Epistle") reading for Zacchaeus Sunday is [1 Timothy 4:9–15](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/1_Timothy#4:9 "s:Bible (King James)/1 Timothy"), which in and of itself has no Lenten theme, other than as an admonition to righteous behaviour.

#### Publican and Pharisee

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Publican and Pharisee")]

The reading on the Sunday which concludes this week is the [Parable](/wiki/Parables_of_Jesus "Parables of Jesus") of the [Publican and the Pharisee](/wiki/Publican_and_the_Pharisee "Publican and the Pharisee") ([Luke 18:10–14](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Luke#18:10 "s:Bible (King James)/Luke")). The Sunday of the Publican and the Pharisee is the first day the Lenten Triodion is used (at [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") or [All-Night Vigil](/wiki/All-Night_Vigil "All-Night Vigil") on Saturday night), though it is only used for the Sunday services, with nothing pertaining to weekdays or Saturday. The theme of the hymns and readings on this Sunday is dedicated to the lessons to be learned from the parable: that righteous actions alone do not lead to salvation, that pride renders good deeds fruitless, that God can only be approached through a spirit of humility and repentance, and that God [justifies](/wiki/Justification_(theology) "Justification (theology)") the humble rather than the self-righteous. The week which follows the Sunday of the Publican and the Pharisee is a fast-free week, to remind the faithful not to be prideful in their fasting as the Pharisee was ([Luke 18:12](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Luke#18:12 "s:Bible (King James)/Luke")).

The Sunday of the Publican and the Pharisee is also the first day that *structural* changes (as opposed to simply substituting Lenten hymns for normal hymns from the [octoechos](/wiki/Octoechos_(liturgy) "Octoechos (liturgy)") or [menaion](/wiki/Menaion "Menaion")) are made to the Sunday services. For example, there begins to be a significant 'split' after the Great Prokimenon at [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") that night. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Prodigal Son

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Prodigal Son")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Rembrandt_Harmensz_van_Rijn_-_Return_of_the_Prodigal_Son_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Rembrandt_Harmensz_van_Rijn_-_Return_of_the_Prodigal_Son_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rembrandt_Harmensz_van_Rijn_-_Return_of_the_Prodigal_Son_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

[Rembrandt](/wiki/Rembrandt "Rembrandt")'s painting of the *Return of the [Prodigal Son](/wiki/Prodigal_Son "Prodigal Son")* (c. 1662)

The theme of this week is the Parable of the [Prodigal Son](/wiki/Prodigal_Son "Prodigal Son") ([Luke 15:11–32](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Luke#15:11 "s:Bible (King James)/Luke")). Again, the Triodion does not give [propers](/wiki/Proper_(liturgy) "Proper (liturgy)") for the weekdays. The Gospel Reading on Sunday lays out one of the most important themes of the Lenten season: the process of falling into sin, realization of one's sinfulness, the road to repentance, and finally reconciliation, each of which is illustrated in the course of the parable.

The week following is the only week of the Triodion on which there is normal fasting (i.e. no meat, fish, wine, oil or animal products on Wednesday or Friday except if an important feast such as the [Presentation of Jesus at the Temple](/wiki/Presentation_of_Jesus_at_the_Temple "Presentation of Jesus at the Temple") or [Forty Martyrs of Sebaste](/wiki/Forty_Martyrs_of_Sebaste "Forty Martyrs of Sebaste") falls on that day, in which case certain foods are allowed).

#### Meatfare Week

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Meatfare Week")]

The Saturday of this week is the first [Saturday of the Dead](/wiki/Saturday_of_the_Dead "Saturday of the Dead") observed during the Great Lenten season. The proper name in the [typikon](/wiki/Typikon "Typikon") for the Sunday of this week is *The Sunday of the [Last Judgment](/wiki/Last_Judgment "Last Judgment")*, indicating the theme of the Gospel of the day ([Matthew 25:31–46](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#25:31 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew")). The popular name of "Meatfare Sunday" comes from the fact that this is the last day on which the laity are permitted to eat meat until Pascha (Byzantine Rite monks and nuns never eat meat).

#### Cheesefare Week

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Cheesefare Week")]

Main article: [Cheesefare Week](/wiki/Cheesefare_Week "Cheesefare Week")

During Cheesefare Week the eating of dairy products is permitted on every day (even Wednesday and Friday, which are normally observed as fast days throughout the year), though meat may no longer be eaten any day of the week. On the weekdays of this week, the first Lenten structural elements are introduced to the cycle of services on weekdays (the chanting of "Alleluia", the Prayer of Saint Ephrem, making prostrations, etc.).

Wednesday and Friday are the most lenten, but some lenten elements are also observed on Monday, Tuesday and Thursday. The office of Cheesefare Saturday celebrates the "Holy Ascetic Fathers".

Cheesefare Week is concluded on [Cheesefare Sunday](/wiki/Cheesefare_Sunday "Cheesefare Sunday"). The proper name for this Sunday is *The Sunday of Forgiveness*, both because of the Gospel theme for the day ([Matthew 6:14–21](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#6:14 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew")) and because it is the day on which everyone asks forgiveness of their neighbor. The popular name of "Cheesefare Sunday" derives from the fact that it is the last day to eat dairy products before Pascha. On this Sunday, Eastern Christians identify with [Adam and Eve](/wiki/Adam_and_Eve "Adam and Eve"), and forgive each other in order to obtain forgiveness from God, typically in a Forgiveness Vespers service that Sunday evening.

During Forgiveness Vespers (on Sunday evening) the [hangings](/wiki/Antependia "Antependia") and [vestments](/wiki/Vestments "Vestments") in the church are changed to somber Lenten colours to reflect a penitential mood. At the end of the service comes the "Ceremony of Mutual Forgiveness" during which all of the people one by one ask forgiveness of one another, that the Great Fast may begin in a spirit of peace.

### The Great Forty Days

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: The Great Forty Days")]

The forty days of Great Lent last from Clean Monday until the Friday of the Sixth Week. Each of the Sundays of Great Lent has its own special commemoration, though these are not necessarily repeated during the following week. However, during the Third, Fifth and Sixth Weeks, the idiomelon at Vespers is based on the same parable that the Canon at Matins the previous Sunday was (the Prodigal Son, the Good Samaritan and Lazarus and the Rich Man respectively). An exception is the Week of the Cross (the Fourth Week), during which the theme of the preceding Sunday—the [Veneration of the Cross](/wiki/Veneration_of_the_Cross "Veneration of the Cross")—is repeated throughout the week. The themes introduced in the Pre-Lenten period continue to be developed throughout the forty days.

#### Clean Week

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Clean Week")]

The first week of Great Lent starting on [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday"), the first day of Great Lent. The name "Clean Week" refers to the spiritual cleansing each of the faithful is encouraged to undergo through fasting, prayer, repentance, reception of the Holy Mysteries and begging forgiveness of his neighbor. It is also traditionally a time for [spring cleaning](/wiki/Spring_cleaning "Spring cleaning") so that one's outward surroundings matches his inward disposition.

Throughout this week fasting is most strict. Those who have the strength are encouraged to fast completely, eating only on Wednesday and Friday evenings, after the Presanctified Liturgy. Those who are unable to keep such a strict fast are encouraged to eat only a little, and then only [xerophagy](/wiki/Xerophagy "Xerophagy") (see [Prodigal Son](#Prodigal_Son)) once a day. On Monday, no food should be eaten at all and only uncooked food on Tuesday and Thursday. Meals are served on Saturday and Sunday, but these are fasting meals at which meat, dairy products and fish are forbidden.

At [Great Compline](/wiki/Great_Compline "Great Compline") during the first four days of the Fast (Monday through Thursday) the [Great Canon](/wiki/Great_Canon "Great Canon") of St. [Andrew of Crete](/wiki/Andrew_of_Crete "Andrew of Crete") is divided into four parts and one part is chanted each night.

The First Saturday is called "St. Theodore Saturday" in honor of St. [Theodore the Recruit](/wiki/Theodore_the_Recruit "Theodore the Recruit"), a 4th-century [martyr](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr"). At the end of the Presanctified Liturgy on Friday (since, liturgically, the day begins at sunset) a special canon to St. Theodore, composed by St. [John of Damascus](/wiki/John_of_Damascus "John of Damascus"), is chanted. Then the priest blesses [kolyva](/wiki/Kolyva "Kolyva") (boiled wheat with honey and raisins) which is distributed to the faithful in commemoration of the following miracle worked by St. Theodore on the First Saturday of Great Lent.

Fifty years after the death of St Theodore, the emperor [Julian the Apostate](/wiki/Julian_the_Apostate "Julian the Apostate") (361-363), as a part of his general policy of [persecution of Christians](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians "Persecution of Christians"), commanded the governor of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") during the first week of Great Lent to sprinkle all the food provisions in the marketplaces with the blood offered to pagan idols, knowing that the people would be hungry after the strict fasting of the first week. St Theodore appeared in a dream to Archbishop [Eudoxius](/wiki/Eudoxius_of_Antioch "Eudoxius of Antioch"), ordering him to inform all the Christians that no one should buy anything at the marketplaces, but rather to eat cooked wheat with honey (kolyva).

The First Sunday of Great Lent is the [Feast of Orthodoxy](/wiki/Feast_of_Orthodoxy "Feast of Orthodoxy"), which commemorates the restoration of the [veneration](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration") of [icons](/wiki/Icon "Icon") after the [Iconoclast](/wiki/Iconoclasm "Iconoclasm") controversy, which is considered to be the triumph of the Church over the last of the great [heresies](/wiki/Heresy "Heresy") which troubled her (all later heresies being simply a rehashing of earlier ones). Before the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") on this day, a special service, known as the "[Triumph of Orthodoxy](/wiki/Triumph_of_Orthodoxy "Triumph of Orthodoxy")" is held in [cathedrals](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral") and major monasteries, at which the *[synodicon](/w/index.php?title=Synodicon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Synodicon (page does not exist)")* (containing [anathemas](/wiki/Anathema "Anathema") against various heresies, and [encomia](/wiki/Encomia "Encomia") of those who have held fast to the Christian faith) is proclaimed.

The theme of the day is the victory of the True Faith over heresy. "This is the victory that overcomes the world, our faith" ([1 John 5:4](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/1_John#5:4 "s:Bible (King James)/1 John")). Also, the icons of the saints bear witness that man, "created in the image and likeness of God" ([Genesis 1:26](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Genesis#1:26 "s:Bible (King James)/Genesis")), may become holy and [godlike](/wiki/Theosis_(Eastern_Orthodox_theology) "Theosis (Eastern Orthodox theology)") through the purification of himself as God's living image.

The First Sunday of Great Lent originally commemorated the [Prophets](/wiki/Prophet "Prophet") such as [Moses](/wiki/Moses "Moses"), [Aaron](/wiki/Aaron "Aaron"), and [Samuel](/wiki/Samuel_(Bible) "Samuel (Bible)"). The Liturgy's [Prokeimenon](/wiki/Prokeimenon "Prokeimenon") and [alleluia](/wiki/Alleluia "Alleluia") verses as well as the [Epistle](/wiki/Epistle "Epistle") (Hebrews 11:24-26,32-40) and [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") ([John 1:43–51](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/John#1:43 "s:Bible (King James)/John")) readings appointed for the day continue to reflect this older usage.

#### Second Week

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Second Week")]

The Second Sunday of Great Lent commemorates [St. Gregory Palamas](/wiki/Gregory_Palamas "Gregory Palamas"), the great defender of the Church's [doctrine](/wiki/Doctrine "Doctrine") of [Hesychasm](/wiki/Hesychasm "Hesychasm") against its attack by [Barlaam of Calabria](/wiki/Barlaam_of_Calabria "Barlaam of Calabria"). The Epistle is Hebrews 1:10-14; 2:1-3 and the Gospel is [Mark 2:1–12](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Mark#2:1 "s:Bible (King James)/Mark")

Throughout this week, and until the Sixth Friday in Lent, one meal may be taken a day with [xerophagy](/wiki/Xerophagy "Xerophagy"). Until the [Sixth Saturday in Lent](/wiki/Lazarus_Saturday "Lazarus Saturday"), Saturday and Sunday fasting remains the same as in the First Week.

#### Third Week

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Third Week")]

The [Veneration of the Cross](/wiki/Veneration_of_the_Cross "Veneration of the Cross") is celebrated on the third Sunday. The veneration comes on this day because it is the midpoint of the forty days. The services for this day are similar to those on the [Great Feast](/wiki/Great_Feast "Great Feast") of the [Exaltation of the Cross](/wiki/Exaltation_of_the_Cross "Exaltation of the Cross") (September 14). During the [All-Night Vigil](/wiki/All-Night_Vigil "All-Night Vigil") the priest brings the cross out into the center of the church, where it is venerated by the clergy and faithful. It remains in the center of the church through Friday of the week following (the Fourth Week of Great Lent).[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The Epistle is [Hebrews 4:14–5:6](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Hebrews#4:14 "s:Bible (King James)/Hebrews") and the Gospel is [Mark 8:34–9:1](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Mark#8:34 "s:Bible (King James)/Mark").

#### Fourth Week

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Fourth Week")]

This week is celebrated as a sort of [afterfeast](/wiki/Afterfeast "Afterfeast") of the Veneration of the Cross, during which some of the hymns from the previous Sunday are repeated each day. On Monday and Wednesday of the Fourth Week, a Veneration of the Cross takes place at the [First Hour](/wiki/Prime_(liturgy) "Prime (liturgy)") (repeating a portion of the service from the All-Night Vigil of the previous Sunday). On Friday of that week, the veneration takes place after the [Ninth Hour](/wiki/Ninth_Hour "Ninth Hour"), after which the cross is solemnly returned to the sanctuary by the priest and deacon.

The Sunday which ends the fourth week is dedicated to [St. John Climacus](/wiki/St._John_Climacus "St. John Climacus"), whose work, *The Ladder of Divine Ascent* has been read throughout the Great Lenten Fast.

#### Fifth Week

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Fifth Week")]

Main article: [Great Canon](/wiki/Great_Canon "Great Canon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Andrew_of_Crete.jpg/250px-Andrew_of_Crete.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrew_of_Crete.jpg)

[Russian Icon](/wiki/Russian_icon "Russian icon") depicting [St. Andrew of Crete](/wiki/Andrew_of_Crete "Andrew of Crete") (left) and [St. Mary of Egypt](/wiki/St._Mary_of_Egypt "St. Mary of Egypt")

On Thursday of the Fifth Week, the [Great Canon](/wiki/Great_Canon "Great Canon") of [St. Andrew of Crete](/wiki/Andrew_of_Crete "Andrew of Crete") is chanted in its entirety during [Orthros](/wiki/Orthros "Orthros"). This is the longest Canon of the church year, and during the course of its nine [Odes](/wiki/Ode "Ode"), most every person mentioned in the Bible is called to mind and tied to the theme of repentance. In anticipation of the Canon, Vespers on Wednesday afternoon is longer than normal, with special [stichera](/wiki/Stichera "Stichera") added in honor of the Great Canon. While the Great Canon itself is recited during [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins") for Thursday, this service is usually celebrated by anticipation on the prior Wednesday evening, so that more people can attend.

As a part of the Matins of the Great Canon, the *Life of St. Mary of Egypt* by St. [Sophronius](/wiki/Sophronius_of_Jerusalem "Sophronius of Jerusalem"), [Patriarch of Jerusalem](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarch_of_Jerusalem "Greek Orthodox Patriarch of Jerusalem") (634 - 638) is read, for her example of repentance and overcoming temptation. On this day also is chanted the famous [kontakion](/wiki/Kontakion "Kontakion"), "My soul, my soul, why sleepest thou..." by St. [Romanos the Melodist](/wiki/Romanos_the_Melodist "Romanos the Melodist"). The next day (Thursday morning) a special Presanctified Liturgy is celebrated, and the fast is relaxed slightly (wine and oil are allowed) as consolation after the long service the night before.

Saturday of the Fifth Week is dedicated to the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos") (Mother of God), and is known as the "Saturday of the Akathist" because the [Akathist](/wiki/Akathist "Akathist") to the Theotokos is prescribed to be chanted during Matins on that day, though as with the Great Canon, usually done in anticipation on Friday evening to allow more people to attend.

The Fifth Sunday is dedicated to [St. Mary of Egypt](/wiki/Mary_of_Egypt "Mary of Egypt"), whose *Life* was read earlier in the week during the Great Canon. At the end of the Divine Liturgy many churches celebrate a "Blessing of [Dried Fruit](/wiki/Dried_fruit "Dried fruit")", in commemoration of St. Mary's profound asceticism.

#### Sixth Week

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Sixth Week")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/%D0%92%D0%BE%D1%81%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B5_%D0%9B%D0%B0%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%8F2.jpg/250px-%D0%92%D0%BE%D1%81%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B5_%D0%9B%D0%B0%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%8F2.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%92%D0%BE%D1%81%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%88%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B5_%D0%9B%D0%B0%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%8F2.jpg)

Icon of the [Raising of Lazarus](/wiki/Lazarus_Saturday "Lazarus Saturday") (15th century, [Novgorod school](/wiki/Novgorod_school "Novgorod school")). In most [icons](/wiki/Icons "Icons") of [death](/wiki/Death "Death"), [resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection "Resurrection") and [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"), the gates of [hades](/wiki/Christian_views_on_Hades "Christian views on Hades") are visible in the background, as they are here.

During the Sixth Week the Lenten services are served as they were during the second and third weeks. However, the hymns of each day begin to take on a more narrative form (as they will during Holy Week) in anticipation of the celebration of Lazarus Saturday.

Great Lent ends at Vespers on the evening of the Sixth Friday, and the Lenten cycle of Old Testament readings is brought to an end. (Genesis ends with the account of the burial of Joseph, who is a [type](/wiki/Typology_(theology) "Typology (theology)") of Christ.) At that same service, the celebration of [Lazarus Saturday](/wiki/Lazarus_Saturday "Lazarus Saturday") begins. The [resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection "Resurrection") of [Lazarus](/wiki/Lazarus_of_Bethany "Lazarus of Bethany") is understood as a foreshadowing of the [Resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus"), and many of the Resurrection hymns normally chanted on Sunday (and which will be replaced the next day with hymns for Palm Sunday) are chanted at Matins on the morning of Lazarus Saturday.

[Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") differs from the previous Sundays in that it is one of the [Great Feasts of the Orthodox Church](/wiki/Great_Feasts_of_the_Orthodox_Church "Great Feasts of the Orthodox Church"). None of the normal Lenten material is chanted on Palm Sunday, and fish, wine and oil are permitted in the [trapeza](/wiki/Refectory "Refectory"). The blessing of palms (or pussywillow) takes place at Matins on Sunday morning, and everyone stands holding palms and lit candles during the important moments of the service.

This is especially significant at the [Great Entrance](/wiki/Great_Entrance "Great Entrance") during the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") on Palm Sunday morning, since liturgically that entrance recreates the entry of Jesus into Jerusalem. The themes of Lazarus Saturday and Palm Sunday are tied together, and some of the same hymns (including one of the [apolytikia](/wiki/Apolytikion "Apolytikion")) are chanted on both days. The Holy Week services begin on the night of Palm Sunday, and the [liturgical colours](/wiki/Liturgical_colours "Liturgical colours") are changed from the festive hues of Lazarus Saturday and Palm Sunday back to somber Lenten colours.

### Holy Week

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Holy Week")]

Main article: [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week")

Although technically, Holy Week is separate from Great Lent, its services mirror those of Great Lent and are contained in the same book, the *Lenten Triodion*. Whereas, during Great Lent each week has its own theme, during Holy Week each day has its own theme, again based upon the Gospel readings for the day:

* [Holy and Great Monday](/wiki/Holy_and_Great_Monday "Holy and Great Monday")—[Joseph the all-comely](/wiki/Joseph_the_all-comely "Joseph the all-comely") as a [type](/wiki/Typology_(theology) "Typology (theology)") of Christ, and the account of [The Fig Tree](/wiki/Parable_of_the_budding_fig_tree "Parable of the budding fig tree") ([Matthew 21:18–22](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#21:18 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew"))
* [Holy and Great Tuesday](/wiki/Holy_and_Great_Tuesday "Holy and Great Tuesday")—the [Parable of the Ten Virgins](/wiki/Parable_of_the_Ten_Virgins "Parable of the Ten Virgins") ([Matthew 25:1–13](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#25:1 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew"))
* [Holy and Great Wednesday](/wiki/Holy_and_Great_Wednesday "Holy and Great Wednesday")—The [anointing of Jesus](/wiki/Anointing_of_Jesus "Anointing of Jesus") at [Bethany](/wiki/Bethany_(Biblical_village) "Bethany (Biblical village)") ([Matthew 26:6–16](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#26:6 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew"))

Note that for the previous three days, one meal a day is taken a day with xerophagy.

* [Holy and Great Thursday](/wiki/Holy_and_Great_Thursday "Holy and Great Thursday")—The [Mystical Supper](/wiki/Mystical_Supper "Mystical Supper")

One meal may be eaten on this day with wine and oil.

* [Holy and Great Friday](/wiki/Holy_and_Great_Friday "Holy and Great Friday")—The [Passion](/wiki/Passion_(Christianity) "Passion (Christianity)") ([Matthew 27:62–66](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#27:62 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew"))

No food is to be eaten on this day.

* [Holy and Great Saturday](/wiki/Holy_and_Great_Saturday "Holy and Great Saturday")—The [Burial of Jesus](/wiki/Holy_Sepulchre "Holy Sepulchre") and the [Harrowing of Hell](/wiki/Harrowing_of_Hell "Harrowing of Hell") ([Matthew 28:1–20](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#28:1 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew"))

One meal may be eaten with xerophagy.

During Holy Week, the order of services is often brought forward by several hours: Matins being celebrated by anticipation the evening before, and Vespers in the morning. This "reversal" is not something mandated by the [typicon](/wiki/Typicon "Typicon") but has developed out of practical necessity. Since some of the most important readings and liturgical actions take place at Matins, it is celebrated in the evening (rather than early in the morning before dawn, as is usual for Matins) so that more people can attend.

Since during Holy Week Vespers is usually joined to either the Presanctified Liturgy or the Divine Liturgy, and since the faithful must observe a total fast from all food and drink before receiving [Holy Communion](/wiki/Holy_Communion "Holy Communion"), it is celebrated in the morning. Vespers on Good Friday is an exception to this, usually being celebrated in late morning or in the afternoon.

The Matins services for Holy Monday through Thursday are referred to as "Bridegroom Prayer" because the [troparion](/wiki/Troparion "Troparion") of the day and the [exapostilarion](/wiki/Exapostilarion "Exapostilarion") (the hymn that concludes the [Canon](/wiki/Canon_(hymnography) "Canon (hymnography)")) develop the theme of "Christ the Bridegroom". Thursday has its own troparion, but uses the same exapostilarion. The [icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") often displayed on these days depicts Jesus and is referred to as "the Bridegroom" because the [crown of thorns](/wiki/Crown_of_thorns "Crown of thorns") and the [robe](/wiki/Robe "Robe") of mockery are parallel to the crown and robe worn by a bridegroom on his wedding day.

This icon is often confused with the visually similar icon of Christ as the [Man of Sorrows](/wiki/Man_of_Sorrows "Man of Sorrows"), which shows Him post-Crucifixion in the same pose but lacking the rod and robe, dead, showing the marks of the nails in his Hands and the spear wound in His side. Incidentally, Thursday has its own icon showing either the [Mystical Supper](/wiki/Mystical_Supper "Mystical Supper") or the [Washing of Feet](/wiki/Washing_of_Feet "Washing of Feet"), or both. The Passion of Christ is seen as the [wedding](/wiki/Wedding "Wedding") of the Saviour with his bride, the Church.

#### Holy Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Holy Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/5208-20080122-1255UTC--jerusalem-calvary.jpg/250px-5208-20080122-1255UTC--jerusalem-calvary.jpg)](/wiki/File:5208-20080122-1255UTC--jerusalem-calvary.jpg)

[Icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") of Christ *The Bridegroom* (Ό Νυμφίος) at Golgotha in the [Church of the Holy Sepulchre](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Sepulchre "Church of the Holy Sepulchre")

The first three days of Holy Week (Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday), the services all follow the same pattern and are nearly identical to the order followed on weekdays during the Great Forty Days; however, the number of [Kathismata](/wiki/Kathisma "Kathisma") (sections from the Psalter) is reduced and the Old Testament readings are taken from different books. The Presanctified Liturgy is celebrated on each of the first three days, and there is a Gospel reading at each one (during the Forty Days there was no Gospel reading unless it was a feast day). There is also a Gospel reading at [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins") on each day and the [Canon](/wiki/Canon_(hymnography) "Canon (hymnography)") chanted at Matins is much shorter, consisting of only three or four odes rather than the usual nine.

In addition to the Gospel readings at Matins and Vespers, there is a reading of all four Gospels which takes place during the [Little Hours](/wiki/Little_Hours "Little Hours") ([Third Hour](/wiki/Third_Hour "Third Hour"), [Sixth Hour](/wiki/Sext "Sext") and [Ninth Hour](/wiki/Ninth_Hour "Ninth Hour")) on these first three days. Each Gospel is read in its entirety and in order, beginning with [Matthew 1:1](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#1:1 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew"), and continuing through [John 13:30](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/John#13:30 "s:Bible (King James)/John") (the rest of the Gospel of John will be read during the remainder of Holy Week). The Gospels are divided up into nine sections with one section being read by the priest at each of the Little Hours.

The [Prayer of Saint Ephrem](/wiki/Prayer_of_Saint_Ephrem "Prayer of Saint Ephrem") is said for the last time at the end of the Presanctified Liturgy on Holy and Great Wednesday. From this moment on, there will be no more prostrations made in the church (aside from those made before the [epitaphios](/wiki/Epitaphios_(liturgical) "Epitaphios (liturgical)")) until Vespers on the afternoon of [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost").

In some churches, the [Holy Mystery](/wiki/Holy_Mystery "Holy Mystery") (Sacrament) of [Unction](/wiki/Unction "Unction") is celebrated on Holy and Great Wednesday, in commemoration of the [anointing](/wiki/Anointing "Anointing") of Jesus' feet in preparation for his burial ([Matthew 26:6–13](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#26:6 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew")).

The [remaining three days](/wiki/Triduum "Triduum") of Holy Week retain a smaller degree of Lenten character, but each has elements that are unique to it.

#### Holy (Maundy) Thursday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Holy (Maundy) Thursday")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Last_Supper_by_Theophanes_the_Cretan.jpg/250px-Last_Supper_by_Theophanes_the_Cretan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Last_Supper_by_Theophanes_the_Cretan.jpg)

*[Mystical Supper](/wiki/Mystical_Supper "Mystical Supper")* by Theophanes the Cretan (16th century, Stavronikita monastery, Mount Athos)

**Holy and Great Thursday** is a more festive day than the others of Holy Week in that it celebrates the institution of the Eucharist. The [hangings](/wiki/Antependia "Antependia") in the church and the [vestments](/wiki/Vestment "Vestment") of the clergy are changed from dark Lenten hues to more festive colours (red, in the Russian tradition).

Whereas the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") is forbidden on other Lenten weekdays, the [Divine Liturgy of St. Basil](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy_of_St._Basil "Divine Liturgy of St. Basil") (combined with Vespers) is celebrated on this day. Many of the standard hymns of the Liturgy are replaced with the [Troparion](/wiki/Troparion "Troparion") of Great Thursday. In some churches, the [Holy Table](/wiki/Holy_Table "Holy Table") (altar) is covered with a simple white linen cloth, in commemoration of the [Mystical Supper](/wiki/Mystical_Supper "Mystical Supper") (Last Supper).

During this Divine Liturgy, the [reserved Mysteries](/wiki/Reserved_Mysteries "Reserved Mysteries") are renewed (a new [Lamb](/wiki/Lamb_(liturgy) "Lamb (liturgy)") being [consecrated](/wiki/Consecrated "Consecrated"), and the old Body and Blood of Christ being consumed by the deacon after the Liturgy). Also, when the supply of [Chrism](/wiki/Chrism "Chrism") runs low, it is at this Liturgy that the heads of the [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephalous "Autocephalous") churches will Sanctify new Chrism,[[15]](#cite_note-15) the preparation of which would have been begun during the [All-Night Vigil](/wiki/All-Night_Vigil "All-Night Vigil") on Palm Sunday.

After the Liturgy, a meal is served. The rule of [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting") is lessened somewhat, and the faithful are allowed to partake of wine in moderation during the meal and use oil in the cooking.

That night, the hangings and vestments in the church are changed to black, and Matins for Great and Holy Friday is celebrated.

#### Good Friday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Good Friday")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Great_and_Holy_Friday_Matins.jpg/250px-Great_and_Holy_Friday_Matins.jpg)](/wiki/File:Great_and_Holy_Friday_Matins.jpg)

A [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins") service, before the [tomb](/wiki/Holy_Sepulchre "Holy Sepulchre") on Good Friday at a [church in Chicago](/wiki/St._Joseph_the_Betrothed_Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "St. Joseph the Betrothed Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church")

**Holy and Great Friday** is observed as a strict fast day, on which the faithful who are physically able to should not eat anything at all. Some even fast from water, at least until after the Vespers service that evening.

The Matins service, usually celebrated Thursday night, is officially entitled, "The Office of the Holy and Redeeming Passion of our Lord Jesus Christ".[[16]](#cite_note-16) It is commonly known as the "Matins of the Twelve Gospels", because interspersed throughout the service are twelve Gospel readings which recount the entire [Passion of Christ](/wiki/Passion_(Christianity) "Passion (Christianity)") from the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") to the sealing of the [tomb](/wiki/Holy_Sepulchre "Holy Sepulchre"). Before the Sixth Gospel ([Mark 15:16–32](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Mark#15:16 "s:Bible (King James)/Mark")) which first mentions the [Crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion "Crucifixion"), the priest carries a large cross into the center of the church, where it is set upright and all the faithful come forward to venerate it. The cross has attached to it a large [icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") of the *soma* (the crucified body of Christ).

At the beginning of each Gospel, the bell is rung according to the number of the Gospel (once for the first Gospel, two for the second, etc.). As each Gospel is read the faithful stand holding lighted candles, which are extinguished at the end of each reading. After the twelfth Gospel, the faithful do not extinguish their candles but leave them lit and carry the flame to their homes as a blessing. There, they will often use the flame to light the [lampada](/wiki/Lampada "Lampada") in their [icon corner](/wiki/Icon_corner "Icon corner").

On the morning of Great Friday, the [Royal Hours](/wiki/Royal_Hours "Royal Hours") are served. This is a solemn service of the [Little Hours](/wiki/Little_Hours "Little Hours") and [Typica](/wiki/Typica "Typica") to which [antiphons](/wiki/Antiphon "Antiphon"), and scripture readings have been added. Some of the fixed psalms which are standard to each of the Little Hours are replaced with psalms which are of particular significance to the Passion.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/TzanesIcon.jpg/250px-TzanesIcon.jpg)](/wiki/File:TzanesIcon.jpg)

*[Epitaphios](/wiki/Epitaphios_(liturgical) "Epitaphios (liturgical)")* (c. 1600, Emmanuel Tzanes Bounialis, [Cretan School](/wiki/Cretan_School "Cretan School"))

Vespers on Good Friday is usually celebrated after the Royal Hours service,[[17]](#cite_note-17) although in some monasteries it is served in the afternoon.[[18]](#cite_note-18) After the [Little Entrance](/wiki/Little_Entrance "Little Entrance") the Gospel reading is a [concatenation](/wiki/Concatenation "Concatenation") of the [four Evangelists](/wiki/Four_Evangelists "Four Evangelists")' accounts of the Crucifixion and the [Descent from the Cross](/wiki/Descent_from_the_Cross "Descent from the Cross"). At the point during the reading which mentions [Joseph of Arimathea](/wiki/Joseph_of_Arimathea "Joseph of Arimathea") and [Nicodemus](/wiki/Nicodemus "Nicodemus"), two clergymen approach the large cross in the center of the church, remove the *soma*, wrap it in a piece of white linen, and carry it into the sanctuary.

Later, during the [Troparion](/wiki/Troparion "Troparion"), the clergy carry the [epitaphios](/wiki/Epitaphios_(liturgical) "Epitaphios (liturgical)") (a cloth icon symbolizing the [winding sheet](/wiki/Winding_sheet "Winding sheet") in which Jesus was prepared for burial) into the center of the church, where it is venerated by all the faithful. Special chants and prayers and chanted along with biblical readings and [psalms](/wiki/Psalms "Psalms") chanted.

That night, the Matins of Lamentation is normally celebrated in the evening. At this service, special hymns and prayers are chanted. The Lamentations of Great and Holy Friday are the main chants of the service. The Lamentation Praises are chanted to very movingly beautiful ancient tones and words which reflect the lament of the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos") over her son [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ"). The [epitaphios](/wiki/Epitaphios_(liturgical) "Epitaphios (liturgical)") is placed on a beautifully ornate and decorate [catafalque](/wiki/Catafalque "Catafalque") or [bier](/wiki/Bier "Bier") before the Lamentations representing the tomb of Christ.

The priest then sprinkles [rosewater](/wiki/Rosewater "Rosewater") and fresh rose petals all over the tomb, the congregation, and the temple/church. A procession with the ornate tomb then takes place around the church and back into the church where it will be venerated by everyone. As more special prayers and chants are sung especially the chant: "The Noble Joseph..." as the service finishes.

#### Holy Saturday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Great_Lent&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Holy Saturday")]

**Holy and Great Saturday** (known also as the **Great Sabbath**, because on it Jesus "rested" from his labours on the Cross) combines elements of deep sorrow and exultant joy. This, like Good Friday is also a day of strict fasting, though a meal may be served after the Divine Liturgy at which wine (but not oil) may be used.

The Matins of Lamentation (usually celebrated on Friday evening) resembles the Byzantine Rite [funeral](/wiki/Funeral "Funeral") service, in that its main component is the chanting of [Psalm 118](/wiki/Psalm_119 "Psalm 119") (the longest Psalm in the Bible), each verse of which is interspersed with laudations (*ainoi*) of the dead Christ. The service takes place with the clergy and people gathered around the epitaphios in the center of the church. Everyone stands holding lighted candles during the psalm. Next are chanted the *[Evlogitaria](/wiki/Evlogitaria "Evlogitaria") of the Resurrection*, hymns which are normally chanted only on Sundays.

This is the first liturgical mention of the impending [Resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus"). At the end of the [Great Doxology](/wiki/Great_Doxology "Great Doxology") the epitaphios is carried in [procession](/wiki/Procession#Eastern_Orthodox "Procession") around the outside of the church, as is the body at a priest's funeral, and then is brought back in. By local custom, the clergy may raise the epitaphios at the door so that all may pass under it as they enter in, symbolically entering into the death and resurrection of Jesus. The Gospel ([Matthew 27:62–66](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#27:62 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew")) is not read at its usual place during Matins, but rather, following readings of the vision in [Ezekiel](/wiki/Book_of_Ezekiel "Book of Ezekiel") of the dry bones returning to life and an Epistle, near the end of the service, in front of the epitaphios. This positioning is a remnant of a pre-Byzantine rite celebrated at Jerusalem.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Russian_Resurrection_icon.jpg/250px-Russian_Resurrection_icon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russian_Resurrection_icon.jpg)

A 16th-century [Russian icon](/wiki/Russian_icon "Russian icon") of the descent into Hades of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ"), which is the icon for Holy and Great Saturday

The next morning (Saturday), the [Divine Liturgy of St. Basil](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy_of_St._Basil "Divine Liturgy of St. Basil") is celebrated (combined with [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers")). At the beginning of the service, the hangings and vestments are still black. The service is much longer than usual, and includes 15 Old Testament readings recounting the history of salvation, including two [canticles](/wiki/Canticles "Canticles"), the [Song of Moses](/wiki/Song_of_the_Sea "Song of the Sea") and the [Song of the Three Holy Children](/wiki/Song_of_the_Three_Holy_Children "Song of the Three Holy Children"), and showing [types](/wiki/Typology_(theology) "Typology (theology)") of the death and resurrection of Jesus.

Many parts of the liturgy which are normally performed in front of the [Holy Doors](/wiki/Holy_Doors "Holy Doors") are instead done in front of the epitaphios. Just before the Gospel reading, the hangings and vestments are changed to white, and the entire atmosphere of the service is transformed from sorrow to joy. In the Greek practice, the priest strews the entire church with fresh [bay leaves](/wiki/Bay_leaf "Bay leaf"), symbolizing Christ's victory over death. This service symbolizes the descent of Christ into [Hades](/wiki/Hades_in_Christianity "Hades in Christianity") and the [Harrowing of Hell](/wiki/Harrowing_of_Hell "Harrowing of Hell").

Thus, according to Byzantine Rite theology, Jesus' salvific work on the Cross has been accomplished, and the righteous departed in the [Bosom of Abraham](/wiki/Bosom_of_Abraham "Bosom of Abraham") have been released from their bondage; however, the Good News of the Resurrection has not yet been proclaimed to the living on earth, the celebration of which commences at midnight with Matins. For this reason, neither is the fast broken nor the [Paschal greeting](/wiki/Paschal_greeting "Paschal greeting") exchanged.

At the end of the Divine Liturgy, the priest blesses wine and bread which are distributed to the faithful. This is different from the [Sacred Mysteries](/wiki/Sacred_Mysteries "Sacred Mysteries") (Holy Communion) which were received earlier in the service. This bread and wine are simply [blessed](/wiki/Blessing "Blessing"), not [consecrated](/wiki/Consecrated "Consecrated"). They are a remnant of the ancient tradition of the church (still observed in some places) whereby the faithful did not leave the church after the service, but were each given a glass of [wine](/wiki/Wine "Wine"), and some [bread](/wiki/Bread "Bread") and [dried fruit](/wiki/Dried_fruit "Dried fruit") to give them strength for the vigil ahead. They would listen to the reading of the [Acts of the Apostles](/wiki/Acts_of_the_Apostles "Acts of the Apostles"), read in full, and await the beginning of the [Paschal Vigil](/wiki/Paschal_Vigil "Paschal Vigil"). However, this is not usually done nowadays.

The last liturgical service in the Lenten Triodion is the [Midnight Office](/wiki/Midnight_Office "Midnight Office") which forms the first part of the Paschal Vigil. During this service the [Canon](/wiki/Canon_(hymnography) "Canon (hymnography)") of Great Saturday is repeated, near the end of which, during the ninth ode, the priest and deacon take the epitaphios into the sanctuary through the [Holy Doors](/wiki/Holy_Doors "Holy Doors") and lay it on the [Holy Table](/wiki/Holy_Table "Holy Table") (altar), where it remains until the feast of the [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus"). After the concluding prayers and a [dismissal](/wiki/Dismissal_(liturgy) "Dismissal (liturgy)"), all of the lights and candles in the church are extinguished, and all wait in silence and darkness for the stroke of midnight, following which the [Pentecostarion](/wiki/Pentecostarion "Pentecostarion") replaces the Lenten Triodion, commencing with the resurrection of Christ being proclaimed.
